key_paragraphs.text: PROGRESS ARTICLE Attosecond science The motion of electrons on the atomic scale has been hidden from direct experimental access until recently. We review the revolution in technology that opened the door to real-time observation and time-domain control of atomic-scale electron dynamics, and address the expected implications of having the tools to monitor electrons with sub-atomic resolution in both space and time. P. B. CORKUM1 AND FERENC KRAUSZ2,3 Step 1: Ionization. When induced by a strong (n

PROGRESS ARTICLE of the optical pulse controls the kinetic energy2, amplitude17 and phase18 of the recollision electron and therefore the attosecond pulse19 that it produces. In addition to producing attosecond electron and photon pulses, the recollision simultaneously encodes all information on the electron interference. Once the amplitude and phase of the electron interference is encoded in light, powerful optical methods become may push the frontiers of attosecond technology near the atomic u

PROGRESS ARTICLE measurement28,29 — follows a diff erent strategy. First a photoelectron replica of the optical pulse is produced and then the electron replica is measured. Although two-photon ionization of atoms allows autocorrelation Time of low-frequency attosecond pulses30,31, at photon energies of 50–100 eV and beyond the streak camera approach19,32 is the method of choice for measuring attosecond pulses: a photoelectron replica is produced and then measured by defl ecting or accelerating i

PROGRESS ARTICLE 3 attoseconds and strong enough to rival the internal forces acting on 2.5 2 2.0 the electrons. Th e electric fi eld of waveform-controlled light provides 1 1.5 such a force. Its ability to control the motion of ionizing electrons 1.0 on a nanometre scale has been demonstrated by the reproducible 0 0.5 0 generation of single and double attosecond photon pulses19. Th e –1 –0.5 controlled electric force of light was recently shown47 to be able also to –2 –1.0 steer the motion of a

PROGRESS ARTICLE the molecule is aligned parallel or perpendicular to the recollision electron, double ionization dynamics is measured48. Th e experiment shows that electrons are much more likely to leave the molecule within a few hundred attoseconds of each other for parallel than for perpendicular molecules. Th e second experiment uses only a single laser pulse, but concentrates on recollision-induced multiple ionization (for example, Ne → Ne4+ + 4e). Only electrons that recollide near the zer
